The way the buffer class works, it yields full lines, as terminated by a LF (or maybe CRLF)... so if the FROM SERVER says that's what it's received, I'm inclined to trust it.

You can troubleshoot by overriding the default buffer behavior and asserting what the incoming bytes are:

```
import sys
from jaraco.stream import buffer
class DebuggingBuffer(buffer.DecodingLineBuffer):
    def feed(self, bytes):
        print("fed ", bytes, file=sys.stderr)
        super(DebuggingBuffer, self).feed(bytes)

irc.client.ServerConnection.buffer_class = DebuggingBuffer
```

Run that somewhere before initiating your connection - then report back the `fed ` prefixed lines.

Hi @Xenthys. I'd recommend instead simply `pip install backports.functools_lru_cache`. That's the import that's missed when `irc` (or `jaraco.functools`) is installed by Setuptools < 0.7 or Pip < 6.0.

As I mentioned above, I was considering updating the setup script to detect when being installed by an old version of setuptools, but I realized that older pip is a much more common scenario and as far as I know, there's no way to hook into pip during an install to check that it is new enough to support the [environment marked dependencies](https://github.com/jaraco/jaraco.functools/blob/master/setup.py#L33-L36).

However, to be more lenient, I've released a new [functools 1.8](http://pythonhosted.org/jaraco.functools/history.html) with more generous handling of a missing `lru_cache` module, which also bypasses this issue.

@gotcha currently working on that with [ipython/ipython#9087](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/9087#issuecomment-166428841). Following down the stack:

1. ipdb makes a [call to Pdb's set_trace](https://github.com/gotcha/ipdb/blob/master/ipdb/__main__.py#L135)
2. [Pdb](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/IPython/core/debugger.py#L201) inherits the unbound set_trace method from [pdb](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/IPython/core/debugger.py#L60)
3. [pdb.set_trace()](https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/2.7/Lib/pdb.py#l1250) [inherits bdb](https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/2.7/Lib/pdb.py#l59)
4. Finally, the call to [bdb.set_trace()](https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/2.7/Lib/bdb.py#l212) is made

One option is to edit `bdb.set_trace()` / [`pdb.print_stack_entrty`](https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/2.7/Lib/pdb.py#l893). Alternatively, we can edit IPython. Either way, I think a simple change to ipdb would be to add a keyword to [set_trace](https://github.com/gotcha/ipdb/blob/master/ipdb/__main__.py#L135) (such as `context=3`) if the method allows it.

> tends to eschew almost everything else that one typically sees in asynchronous programming (e.g., callbacks, event loops, Futures, Protocols, etc.).

I see curio as an intriguing exploration of the hypothesis that the best way to do asynchronous programming is to throw away all those clunky legacy constructs ;-). It's possible it would make sense to at some point add back some kind of interoperability with those other approaches and it would be pretty easy to do given curio as it exists now, but it's interesting to see how far one can get before needing that.
I'm not even sure why that SO_REUSEPORT option is in there in the first place.

You can see for yourself :-): https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/blob/3cc8acbc40fc07404459e87177774de6549aa5ba/curio/task.py#L65-L74

I guess you also need to know that `_join_task` is a kernel trap that blocks until the given task has exited; you can see [its implementation here](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/blob/3cc8acbc40fc07404459e87177774de6549aa5ba/curio/kernel.py#L485-L491).

In this case, `_join_task` will exit immediately because `task.terminated` is true, and then `join` will just return `task2.next_value`. All of these operations are O(1) -- they don't depend on the number of tasks running. It'll be slower than a key lookup via gatherd because `join` is a blocking trap that triggers rescheduling etc., but this is unlikely to matter in practice. (In fact, if the speed of `join` *does* matter, and you really only care about the one task, then you shouldn't be calling `gather`, because if you look at the source for `gather` you'll see that it calls `join` on all of those other tasks that you don't care about.)

Some additional thoughts about your ideas:
Wrapping the tcl scripts with a makefile has its pros and cons. I think you use the approach already with the simulations. The advantage is of course the ability to specify different targets. The disadvantage is that it is not possible to run the makefile out of Vivado tcl console. Currently I start Vivado without opening a project, cd to the build dir, and source the script. When something is wrong, I close the project, rerun fusesoc (which as a side effect cleans the build dir) and then source the script again. This is quite fast because I save the time to load Vivado into memory.

On the other side, when using makefiles, it may be an idea to go further with this concept and for example offer more targets:

* Build in batch
* Create project interactively without running implementation 
* Create project for interactive debug/simulation (in fact this would be more comfortable than having separate build and simulation workflows as today)
* Program bitfile
* ... all sorts of other ideas

So when reasoning about the potentials the makefile approach will pay out eventually.

Sorry for the delay. One way to do a whitelist would be to start with those given in [the format spec](https://nbformat.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format_description.html#metadata), i.e.

- `kernelspec`
- `collapsed`
- `autoscroll`
- `deletable`
- `format`
- `name`
- `tags`
- `isolated`

along with those used by `nbstripout`,

- `init_cell`
- `keep_output`
- `scrolled`

People seem to be using metadata for other various things in the wild (author/description etc.) and it's also used by some extensions (which could register themselves on the whitelist, in theory). 

Having looked at all this I've managed to convince myself a blacklist is a more sensible option (since other metadata doesn't change in a useless way like this does. So I've ended up making a really simple change that just strips this one field (but could trivially do others too).

This is the kind of issue which the new style of Django middleware (coming in 1.10) was designed to prevent (see point 1 of the [behavioural differences](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/http/middleware/#upgrading-middleware) section). The `SolidLocaleMiddleware` is modifying the response, even though `WhiteNoiseMiddleware` has short-circuited the response cycle.

Within the old middleware framework the only way to fix this is to fix `SolidLocaleMiddleware` to not modify responses to requests that it didn't process e.g. by adding a flag like `request._seen_by_solid_locale = True` in the `process_request` method and then checking for the presence of this flag in the `process_response` method.

Regarding the cache hit rate, the thing that really matters here is the `Vary` header as that specifies the circumstances under which cached results can be reused. In the example you sent, the `Vary` header didn't have `content-language` in it, so the presence of the language header shouldn't affect caching behaviour.

There were some other values (`X-Requested-With` and `X-http2`) in the vary header which I wouldn't have expected, but I guess you have some other middleware which is adding those.

According to: https://projects.drogon.net/understanding-spi-on-the-raspberry-pi/

> An important aspect of SPI is that the clock can (usually) be driven at varying speeds – the dependence is usually on the peripheral device being driven. E.g. the analog to digital converter on the Gertboard must be driven at a speed between 1MHz and 3MHz – however the upper limit is the voltage it’s supplied with (3MHz max. at 5v), so it’s important to know in-advance what limitations of the devices you are talking to.
>
> To experiment with timings, I wrote a little test program to see the effects of the different speeds – starting at 0.5Mb/sec going up to 62Mb/sec. I simply looped the MOSI and MISO pins together and checked that the data received was the same as the data sent.
>
> The first thing I found was that the Pi stops sending at clock speeds over 62Mb/sec., and that in-reality 32Mbs is the upper limit of the SPI clock.
>
> Next, I noticed that for some clock speeds, the data rate doesn’t change – this is due to the rounding issues when calculating the clock divider value. What I have observed is that the speeds available are:
> 
> 0.5 MHz
> 1 MHz
> 2 MHz
> 4 MHz
> 8 MHz
> 16 MHz and
> 32 MHz.
>
> You can see the progression, it’s powers of 2, and this reflects the clock divider values too.
>
> The important thing to realise is that if you are clocking a part designed for a SPI clock rate of 25MHz, then setting the clock to 25MHz will really create a clock of 16MHz. There is no way to get an exact 25MHz clock speed. This probably isn’t important, and the reality of trying to send a 25MHz signal down a pair of wires or along a PCB track is that it’s highly likely to not work unless you take good care with the signal routing, shielding and so on.
>
> Another observation was that the overheads in the Linux kernel increase with clock speed and the data throughput doesn’t quite increase linearly with clock speed. So with a clock at 1MHz we get a throughput of 0.108 MB/sec, at 2MHz it’s 0.214 MB/sec and it more or less doubles up to 16MHz (1.55MB/sec) but at 32Mb/sec clock, it’s barely 2.2MB/sec when you might expect it to be closer to 3MB/sec.
>
> Finally, it’s worth while noting that the actual latency of calling the wiringPiSPIDataRW() function is rather high – an example I tested was sending 2 bytes at a time to the Gertboards digital to analog converter and rather than the 50,000 updates/sec I was expecting I was seeing barely half that, and a few experiments shows that it wasn’t that tied to the SPI clock frequency either.

If you are not familiar with collaboration on GitHub / GitLab the process is basically:
you create a branch of the source code (this is now a private copy for you).  You make the software code changes that "fix" or add the feature you are looking for.
You then assemble the software code changes into a "pull request".  The developers can look at the software code changes you made and if they look acceptable, they will "accept" them.  Which then makes your software code part of the code that you originally "branched" off of.

> There are some places that use tuples where a list would be more appropriate (like in __all__).

absolutely not:
```__all__ is defined as a list, this means pep257 cannot reliably detect contents of the __all__ variable, because it can be mutated. Change __all__ to be an (immutable) tuple, to remove this warning. Note, pep257 uses __all__ to detect which definitions are public, to warn if public definitions are missing docstrings. If __all__ is a (mutable) list, pep257 cannot reliably assume its contents. pep257 will proceed assuming __all__ is not mutated.```

> like the repeated Python 2/3 compatibility stuff

I could just as easily have adopted the ``six`` package, but I wanted as few external dependencies as possible. There is only one, ``wcwidth`` for CJK character support which I also maintain.

> Injecting new consts into curses is freaky. Why do that?

To provide an easier path for compatible with people who are converting from curses-based code, which I think is a popular demographic target -- If they already have many LOC referring to curses.KEY_Something, then decide they want the additional KEY_Something blessed provides, they might like to continue using their curses.Key_alias without refactoring all of their code -- But I understand your concern, I'm not a fan of monkeypatching, either. We could definitely figure something else out if you're partial about it.

> get_keyboard_codes() and get_keyboard_sequences() are used for [...] same thing.

One must be able to match by both key **or** value -- to avoid expensive searching by-value, we make two different lists -- one that maps keyboard codes (int) to sequences (str), and another in reverse -- sequence (str) to int.

> SequenceTextWrapper [...] I wonder if all that stuff takes a long time to initialize

On initialization time -- I happen to have a terribly slow OSX VPS running tests -- On this machine, and with the overhead of fork()ing the test runner which i estimate at about 250ms, Terminal() initialization is only an additional 50ms or so. You can see them on this page, sorted by test-time: https://teamcity-master.pexpect.org/viewLog.html?buildTypeId=bt20&buildId=2001&tab=testsInfo&pager.currentPage=15

Terminal.wrap() is most definitely more expensive than textwrap.wrap(), and should be used sparingly -- when you may *only* wrap text *after* it has been rendered with sequences.

I have issue https://github.com/jquast/blessed/issues/29 to address the common algorithm found here and elsewhere into a single one where I hope to make a better attack at speed.

> I really like your name "Sequence".

me too, I find many folks like to say "ansi" or "ansi sequence", i made such mistakes in the past. You'll find such definitions often used, but those things more correctly refer to ECMA-48, and they're just very lucky that their TERM types such as "xterm" happen to also honor ECMA-48 codes, but this is not a guarantee. I do, however, ensure those common sequence regex patterns are injected for all terminals so that they can still be identified by "sequence-awareness" -- as they are so commonly used inappropriately

Similarly, such is done with keyboard sequences -- one would hope "xterm" defines a specific list of keyboard sequences that may be expected -- but unfortunately many terminal emulators report "xterm" or similar but send vt220 or vt102 or rxvt codes, etc.

Most correctly, sequences beginning with CSI (``\x1b[``) are "Control Sequence Inducer", so Sequence is really just a term for CSI (irregardless of whether or not it happens to begin with exactly ``\x1b[``)

I do like that it implies the multibyte-sequence nature of the bytes.  You cannot be guaranteed to read a completed sequences on boundries of a file.read() call -- a common mistake, you must interpret them by a state machine of a stream, just like utf-8.

The data passed to the handler is however not suitable to pass on to consumers of _pynput_, as only platform dependent key codes are passed. To convert these codes to usable data, [`KeyTranslator.__call__`](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/blob/master/lib/pynput/_util/win32.py#L429) is used. I suspect this is the culprit. Possible causes of the error in that case are calls to [ToUnicodeEx](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms646322(v=vs.85).aspx), which is called [here](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/blob/master/lib/pynput/_util/win32.py#L571), [here](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/blob/master/lib/pynput/_util/win32.py#L590) and [here](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/blob/master/lib/pynput/_util/win32.py#L610).

My compromise for the batteries-included approach (which I understand) was adding support for auto-import extensions:  https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/tree/master/pyresttest/ext

These are enumerated here at runtime and loaded: https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/pyresttest/resttest.py#L672

This approach allows you to kind of have your cake and eat it too -- you can use additional libraries for extensions if present, but *also* keep the basic package minimal.

Question: Is it really necessary to allow max 120 characters per line?

- PEP008 recommends 79 chars
- [Google Python Style Guide recommend 80](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html#Line_length)
- [SaltStack style](https://docs.saltstack.com/en/latest/topics/development/conventions/style.html#line-length) prefers to follow PEP008 but allows exceptions as you use (120) chars.
- PyCharm defaults to 120 chars limit

There is also [related SO question on this topic](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/88942/why-does-python-pep-8-specify-a-maximum-line-length-of-79-characters).

As can be seen from my links, 79 or 80 chars per line is often used, but there are others setting the limit to 120.

Thanks for quick reply, here are more details, hopefully it will make it more clear:

We have a Java EE 7 JAX-RS rest endpoint that accepts javax.json.JsonObject as a PUT method parameter. One javax.json.JsonObject contains N amount of javax.json.JsonValue objects which all have a key as a String.

JsonObject structure:
{
key1:{jsonvalue1},
key2:{jsonvalue2},
}

For example, CURL command that works:
curl -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X PUT -i -k -d '{"keyToSomeValue": 5}' http://<host>:<port>/<path>

So the data is really simple json object containing key value pair. For example in this case the ValueType of this JsonValue would be ValueType.NUMBER and value of that JsonValue would be 5. And the key is keyToSomeValue.
JsonObject structure:
{
keyToSomeValue: {5} ,
}

curl -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X PUT -i -k -d '{"keyToSomeValue2": “other value”}' http://<host>:<port>/<path>

JsonObject structure:
{
keyToSomeValue2: {“other value”} ,
}

Now our problem is that we are not able to send this kind of json data using pyswagger library. JsonValue ValueType is always interpreted as ValueType.OBJECT because we are not able to send simple {‘key’:value} data. We are only able to send for example key={json object}.

Using ``app_label = 'cascade_dummy'`` was kind of hack, to prevent Django to create migrations files whenever one changed the configuration of a plugin. This was specially nasty, since changing the configuration in ones project, resulted in a new migration file in cmsplugin_cascade – hence this hack.

However, as you pointed out, then the permission system did not work anymore. I didn't pay a lot of attention to this, because I always worked as administrator. In version 0.12, I now changed the logic. Instead of using a non existing app_label for the proxy models, in a faking migration file, I pretend that these proxy models have already been migrated. Therefore all proxy models now are part of the content types and the permission system. You can now even control in a very fine grained way, which user has which permissions on which plugin.

A downside of this, is that I'll have to fix the content type and permission system while bootstrapping the application, since it can be that some proxy models have been added or removed. This is not implemented yet.

Hi there, and thank you for the feedback, Indeed, `markdown` is not automatically installed with `django-modern-rpc` because it is an optional dependency. You will need markdown package only if you write documentation for your RPC methods with Markdown syntax. You can also decide to write it with reStructured syntax. In such case, you will need to install `docutils`. In both case, you have to set `settings.MODERNRPC_DOC_FORMAT` with value `rst` or `md` to determine which parser will be used to transform methods documentation into HTML. Please check https://github.com/alorence/django-modern-rpc/blob/master/modernrpc/core.py#L167 to see how it works. Note that either `docutils.core` or `markdown` are imported only depending on value of `settings.MODERNRPC_DOC_FORMAT`. By default, the variable is empty, which means no particular parser is used to convert method doc to HTML: ```python return "

The issue I'm fixing with this PR actually caused a bug that I reverted not to get picked up by my service until I restarted it this week (and this has also happened to other people in the past, but they didn't look into why it happened).

At least how our configuration distribution system works is, when you revert a file, its modification time goes back to the original modification time of the version you reverted to. So services using the ConfigurationLoader with the default comparator (=almost all of them) do not pick up changes after a revert (which is super important, since if you reverted something, you're probably fixing a bug and you want it to get picked up asap).

Consider running the "**flask routes**" cli command, you will see a table with 3 columns: **Endpoint**, **Methods**, **Rule**.

```
Endpoint                        Methods                 Rule
------------------------------  ----------------------  --------------------------------------------

run_job                         POST                    /api_prefix/scheduler/jobs/<job_id>/run
```

This is a bit messy but it's a quick wrapper:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad

def D(f,outdim):
    def f_i(i):
        return lambda *args, **kwargs: f(*args,**kwargs)[i]
    def deriv(*args,**kwargs):
        return np.concatenate(
            [grad(f_i(i))(*args,**kwargs)[None,...] for i in xrange(outdim)])
    return deriv
```

```python
A = np.random.randn(3,3)
def test(v):
    return np.dot(A,v)
print D(test,3)(np.ones(3))
```

> How does this work with blackbox algorithms only using fit and still acheive larger-than-memory results?

Good question. The implementation I was proposing would require that the entire dataset fit in memory because it would be passed to `fit`. It would feed progressively larger datasets to `fit`, instead of training for progressively longer with `partial_fit`. Feeding datasets into `fit` is a real use case, especially if `partial_fit` is not implemented.

If `partial_fit` is implemented, these two implementations are the same, at least after reworking the current implementation to pass different chunks to `partial_fit` in each Hyperband bracket (which we should do regardless).

To summarize: these two approaches are the same if the model implements `partial_fit`. The new approach I proposed could be used if the entire train dataset fit in memory. I am inclined to go with the current implementation because it does not require the entire dataset to fit in memory and it's very similar to the other implementation.

> Rather than provide a fraction to keep after every batch, provide a decay rate based on the number of rows.

Can you expand on what you mean by "decay rate based on the number of rows"? I have added a `batch_size` parameter that determines what fraction of the dataset each `partial_fit` call sees. This is supported by the paper, and I'm partway through documenting guidelines on how to choose ``max_iter`` and ``batch_size``.

> Number of initial parameters ... Number of final parameters

By initial parameters you mean the parameters that are being searched? The final parameters for every model are available in `cv_results_`.

> 1 same data in each bracket or should it be random among models in each bracket

I had the same thought yesterday. I need to rework the implementation to have different data for each `partial_fit` call, not to have the same data for multiple `partial_fit` calls.

> 2 There is a lot of metadata floating around

Most of these are required because `cv_results_` requires them and the metadata is the only place to get them (e.g., `mean_fit_time`). At most, I could strip out the `std_*` keys and the `params` key. Future work would require putting the `std_*` keys back in.

> 3 In the period when many-but-not-all models have trained in a bracket what should we do?

You are right on (i) and (ii), the synchronous and asynchronous versions (in which there is no threshold). Re (iii): No work is canceled if a job is promoted and Hyperband later finds out it shouldn't have been promoted.

> 4 In regards to testing data ... training data as testing data before we train on it ... Pick out a few rows of each batch of training data [as test data? That's how I'm reading this.]

The test and train data need to be independent and completely separate. A core idea of cross-validation is to only train on the training set and test on the testing set. The goal of model-selection is to pick the model with the highest cross-validation score in hopes that this cross-validation score is representative of real-world data. Mixing the test and train sets is akin to "teaching to the test", and can allow the model to exactly learn the test dataset.

Aye, we should definitely fix this, and I think @hroncok's suggested resolution is a good one.

`--rm` does not do anything fancy, but simply attempts to remove the whole virtualenv directory. What you see is like caused by some underlying error. Pipenv (which uses `shutil.rmtree` underneath) simply ignores any failures it encountered during the process.

Maybe it would be more user-friendly to use the `onerror` handler in this situation, and omit a warning instead?

I agree the runtime cost would be (too) high. Maybe it’s enough to hijack pip’s message and display something else? Replacing the upgrade suggestion with `pipenv run python -m pip install --upgrade pip`, for example. Pipenv already does this message replacement somewhere, iirc.

For the benefit of any future visitors to this issue, please note it turns out that `virtualenv` seems to have problems with practically any characters other than [A-Za-z0-9], at the time of writing. 

For example, see:
https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/issues/457
https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/issues/994

There is a `match` function. Its docstring also contains a small example code, which can be accessed as follows:

```
help(madx.match)
```

However, its functionality is currently somewhat restricted. In particular, it doesn't support the `global` commands. You can either (a) contribute a patch to fix that (b) write your match code like this

```python
madx.command.match(sequence='cassps')
madx.command.vary(name='kqf', step=0.00001)
...
madx.command.global(sequence='cassps', Q1=26.58)
...
madx.command.endmatch()
```

```
periodically_backup () {
  while true; do
    sleep 45

    local current_time=$(date +"%H:%M")
    if [[ "$current_time" == "$BACKUP_EXECUTION_TIME" ]]; then
      local wale_s3_prefix=$(create_wale_prefix)
      wal-e --s3-prefix="$wale_s3_prefix" backup-push "$DATA_DIRECTORY"
      wal-e --s3-prefix="$wale_s3_prefix" delete --confirm retain 30
    fi
  done
}
```

and then in the script
```
periodically_backup &
```

About your idea with reusing the parameters, I'm not sure which approach is better, but here are a few things:
- Say a user changes his coordinate system somewhere without setting it every time he sets a parameter. Now he might set an 'x' with an angle or something without this being immediately obvious.
- From the code it will not be very clear what is happening when there is `setpoint_r2(0.5)`
- I personally prefer an interface (what the parameters actually do) that is static instead of dynamic if it is not absolutely neccesary (for some intruments the state matters a lot, but here we can still do everything simultaniously)

The `BUILD_INTERFACE` for Python includes and libraries is needed because we don't want to hardcode a specific Python version/distribution with the installed pybind11. That would require multiple pybind11 installations -- one for each Python variant. Instead, we defer Python selection to configuration time and append the required properties in [pybind11Config.cmake](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/cca20a7f8d980dd9de2c1054076a7da6b51b5b5d/tools/pybind11Config.cmake.in#L87).

So `BUILD_INTERFACE` definitely needs to stay as it is. Reverting that commit would also resolve the error on Travis.

Regarding the second part, I assume the intended usage from the point of view of the parent project is something like:
```cmake
set(PYBIND11_INSTALL ON)
set(PYBIND11_INSTALL_EXPORT OFF)
add_subdirectory(pybind11)
install(EXPORT pybind11Targets ...)
```
Is this correct?

What's the intended usage for `PYBIND11_EXPORT_NAME`? It seem like the `install(EXPORT <export-name> FILE <name>.cmake)` command should make it possible to rename the export. We'd like to keep the number of configuration variables to a minimum. If this works for you, removing `PYBIND11_EXPORT_NAME` would be preferable.

The many-many approach though, is used by Karpathy in his latest cvpr paper, [found here in section 3](http://cs.stanford.edu/people/karpathy/cvpr2015.pdf).  

re: interpretation discussion. 

Yep!  Though, the one-to-many should be easy with Lasagne, I think, because it's just the initial hidden layer.  The many-to-one is easy, as you said, with the slice layer.  In peaking around at other implementations, it seems most people do this because it is easier.  I haven't found many yet that utilize the error at every time step in the BPTT.

In the paper I linked, Karpathy uses the output at each time step as the embedde d vector for that input word.  He uses that embedded vector along with an image vector and minimizes a structured margin loss function using cosine similarity of the word vector and the image vector(s).  

But, that's not the only error you could have for each time step.  You could also image that at each time step, you have a final layer of sigmoid activations treated as P(next word|words so far), which is more like a traditional chain-emission model (linear crf, hmm, etc).  

Both of the things I described require backpropping the error back at each time step.  I'm also just beginning with RNNs and Lasagne, so I'm not sure if this is possible with how Lasagne is set up.

Hi @mgrep ,

Let's Encrypt by policy never issues any certificates for IP addresses (whether IPv4 or IPv6), only for domain names. This has always been true since Let's Encrypt started operations. See for example

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/certificate-for-public-ip-without-domain-name/6082

and some other forum threads on the same topic.

In this case Certbot actually *did* pass your request through to the certificate authority, which rejected it—the error message

```
{
"type": "urn:ietf:params:acme:error:malformed",
"detail": "Error creating new order :: DNS name does not have enough labels",
"status": 400
}
```

came from the Let's Encrypt CA, not from Certbot. We had a discussion at some point, I think, about whether Certbot should warn users about this case, and I think we decided not to because each CA can decide on its policy for itself. I'll file an issue suggesting that the CA error message should perhaps be more explicit in this case and say something like "We don't issue certificates for IP addresses".

One question I've tossed around is whether NSoT proper should even provide a Web UI. While it would be nice to provide a consistent and useful experience it's not something that has received a lot of attention.

The great things about NSoT is that it has a very extensible data model and is designed with the API in mind first. This means it would be easy for people to build UIs specific to their network workflows or adopt other generic UIs that might be created.

I'd be interested to hear if the current UI (which I am regretfully responsible for) is used very much or if people primarily use the API and CLI tools. If the current UI isn't providing value it might be better to strip it out of the project to remove the complexity.

I do think if we could do it well then it's worth providing a default UI I'm just not certain when that would be a focus.

> I don't like situations when it stops writing, start losing newest data and require additional ops to recover from this state.

This actually results more often from having too many shards per node, rather than too much data on the server.  See https://github.com/elastic/curator/pull/201#issuecomment-59767589

> I can't tell you exactly when that will be with your data and usage pattern, but I will point out that by default, an ES data node will set aside 10% of the heap for indexing (caching, operations, etc.). Each active shard will want no less than 250M of that space. Each inactive shard will want no less than 4MB of that space. Elasticsearch will still force the division to result in values lower than those numbers in the event that this heap space becomes more crowded. If it pushes too far, you will be unable to index. The only way out when this happens is to delete or close indices and usually a node restart. If you need indices to persist beyond this, your options are one or more of the following: a) bigger heap [not to exceed 31G per node], b) closing infrequently accessed indices, c) adding more nodes to the system to rebalance shard count per node.

You have one node.  If you are using the default number of shards per index, which is 5, you have 5 shards multiplied by a rather large number of indices.  You are likely putting a great deal of pressure on that 10% of the heap, perhaps even to the point that it will, as mentioned, stop indexing altogether, until Elasticsearch is restarted.  Any production, time-series use of Elasticsearch should be prepared to be extended horizontally by adding more nodes as needed.  This is the other reason why delete by space is NotGood™, because Elasticsearch should be spread across multiple nodes.
I just noticed that you have 20 shards + 1 replica, which translates into 40 shards per index.  Why are you going with that choice?

The `timeout_override` of the client is sometimes not what's timing out.  Sometimes it's a proxy or a load balancer in front.  You'll be able to see for sure if you set `blacklist: []` in the client config under the `logging` section, and set `loglevel: debug`.  The default hides the `elasticsearch` and `urllib3` responses, and those settings will unhide them.  The timeout is so exactly 60 seconds that it leads me to believe it's a different issue killing it.  But it might not be a proxy or load balancer.  It could even be some code Amazon hacked into their version of Elasticsearch (have they shared the code somewhere? If so you could possibly chase that down).

Regardless, the best troubleshooting you can do is to increase the logging with the provided steps, and see what error code the http request generates.  It will reveal what timed out.

Right, but if I understand, specifying that simply means the client will wait for a max of `timeout` seconds before deciding to raise an error. ES will still happily continue chewing on the query.

The ES timeout is far more important for us here because long running queries can swamp an ES node unless they're killed after some amount of time allowing other queries to run.

Hence the interest in implementing a client-side `msearch` until ES accepts timeout for `_msearch`.

When a timeout happens a request is not retired by default. Typically, especially with `bulk` the timeout means that elasticsearch hasn't been able to perform the required action in time. It does not, however, mean that the action has been cancelled. For `bulk` that means that the documents could have been inserted, retrying can then lead to duplicate documents.

That said, if you wish to override this behavior you can by setting `retry_on_timeout` to `True` and then you can control the number of retries via the `max_retries` parameter to `Elasticsearch` which will be passed to the underlying `Transport` class (0).

I do wonder though if we want to necessarily define a custom plugin loader class, rather than relying on traitlets' built-in functionality. With traitlets, you can define class variables so that instances of the class are loaded, and the type of that instance is configurable. A good example is in nbconvert, where things like the writer and postprocessor are configurable instances: https://github.com/jupyter/nbconvert/blob/master/nbconvert/nbconvertapp.py#L181 e.g. for the writer:

```python
    # Writer specific variables
    writer = Instance('nbconvert.writers.base.WriterBase',  
                      help="""Instance of the writer class used to write the 
                      results of the conversion.""", allow_none=True)
    writer_class = DottedObjectName('FilesWriter', config=True, 
                                    help="""Writer class used to write the 
                                    results of the conversion""")
    writer_aliases = {'fileswriter': 'nbconvert.writers.files.FilesWriter',
                      'debugwriter': 'nbconvert.writers.debug.DebugWriter',
                      'stdoutwriter': 'nbconvert.writers.stdout.StdoutWriter'}
    writer_factory = Type(allow_none=True)

    def _writer_class_changed(self, name, old, new):
        if new.lower() in self.writer_aliases:
            new = self.writer_aliases[new.lower()]
        self.writer_factory = import_item(new)
```

this will by default use the `FilesWriter` class for the writer but it can be customized by passing in any class name. > RandomImpl->getUInt32(void) does not do what one'd expect (uniform random numbers 0..UInt32:max), the samples just a HALF

Nice catch! This means that the random number generator is biased in the same way as [simply using `rand() % n`](http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/cstdlib/rand/). The code that tries to eliminate that bias isn't doing anything.

**So, to summarize the bugs:**

- Either `RandomImpl::getUInt32` needs to return numbers up to ~4 billion, or `Random::getUInt32` needs to do all of its current logic on ~2 billion, not ~4 billion. Until then, our logic that tries to remove bias isn't doing anything.
- After fixing that, there will be a bias toward 0, as described in my first comment. That's also easy to fix.

**Other replies:**

(For these replies, assume `RandomImpl::getUInt32` returns numbers up to ~4 billion.)

- The `do...while` nondeterminism isn't so bad. `smax` is usually near to MAX32 (i.e. 2^32 - 1), and so the probability of needing to retry is low. In the worst case, `smax` is MAX32 / 2, and the probability is 0.5. So in the worst case, the [expected number of tries is 2](http://mrcslws.com/blocks/2015/12/03/how-many-coin-flips-till-heads.html), and it's usually much closer to 1.
- We have the underlying random number generator `RandomImpl` because we want one that is consistent across compilers / platforms. For a given seed, it returns the same sequence of numbers on all platforms.
- The experiment I ran with `RandomImpl` and seed 19 doesn't tell us anything about probability. To quantify the bias, you just need to think about the outer `Random::getUInt32(max)`. As I mentioned in my first comment, if you call it with max=MAX32, the default value, 0 is twice as likely. If you call it with max=1024, then 0 is about 1.000001 times as likely, i.e. 1 millionth more likely.

@deepankar27 thanks for the feedback.
Regarding your first example with `"What will be the weather in San Francisco next week?"`, which dataset did you use for training? It would help to have this information to assess whether the probability is too low or not.

Regarding your second example with `"what is costing book"` and `"what costing book is?"`, what happens here is that the first sentence is parsed by the [`DeterministicIntentParser`](http://snips-nlu.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#snips_nlu.intent_parser.DeterministicIntentParser) because this particular sentence was provided in the training data. When you swap words, you end up with something that has not been seen at train time, thus the `DeterministicParser` does not catch it, and it falls back on the `ProbabilisticIntentParser` which only uses unigram (you can try other combinations of the same words, and you'll get the same probability).

But overall I think you missed something, and I guess it is probably not clear enough. If you try to parse something random like `"bla bli blo"`, you'll see that the NLU engine will output something like:
```
{
  "input": "bla bli blo",
  "intent": None,
  "slots": None
}
```

The NLU engine is trained with an implicit `None` intent, so whenever you got an output which is not None, it means that the output intent is more likely than the `None` intent. So the probability is useful, but normally the NLU engine already does the work of finding the most likely intents amont all your intents + `None`.

Digged further into this. Here's my understanding:
* The failure does indeed result from a second call to `stopProducing` when the certificate validation fails:
  * The first call comes from `twisted.protocols.tls._ProducerMembrane.stopProducing`.
  * The second one comes from `twisted.web._newclient.Request.stopWriting`.
* In this case, treq is using a `FileBodyProducer` from a file-like `BytesIO` object wrapping the post data bytes; this producer is used to deliver the request's body payload.
* `FileBodyProducer` generates the unhandled exception when `stopProducing` is called on it a second time. Motive:
  * It uses a `twisted.internet.task` to cooperatively read bytes from the underlying file and write them to the consumer.
  * When `stopProducing` is called on `FileBodyProducer`, it calls `stop` on its `task` which raises the `TaskStopped` exception when it is already completed in `twisted.internet.task.CooperativeTask._checkFinish`.

So this does seem like a Twisted related issue.

Within my current understanding, addressing this may come down to design decisions around code I'm not totally familiar with:
* Should a TLS wrapped produced have its `stopProducing` method called twice?
* Should calling `stopProducing` twice on `FileBodyProducer` fail?
* Should calling `stop` on a completed `CooperativeTask` fail?

I have no more time to dedicate to this today. My next steps would be:
* Check if there's a bug logged for this aleady in Twisted's bug tracker.
* If not, bring this issue to the Twisted's mailing list.

PS: for some reason this only happens when certificate validation fails; I'm not seeing a single call to `stopProducing` when the request/response cycle is completed successfully. Shouldn't there be one?

@samuelcolvin I think the point of this feature request is to allow adding routes from a routing table to another routing table. A very large project may contain a lot of sub modules, each may have their own sub modules to form a sub module tree. The root project does not know - and should not know about all the descendants, but only the direct children. So if a sub module can "import" the routing table of its own sub module, it makes creating the root routing table easier.

Hi Thibaut,

I am sorry to hear you had issues with that, we are about to release an update of that script to make the creation of custom databases much easier.
You actually identified the problem correctly, it is an inconsistence with the version of the accession numbers. Please try to use the following command to create the file `ftpselection.tsv`, then generate `acc2taxid.tsv` using the command in the docs:
```
awk -F "\t" -v OFS="\t" '$5=="reference genome" {split($1, a, "."); print a[1], $6, $20}' assembly_summary.txt >ftpselection.tsv
cut -f 1,2 ftpselection.tsv >acc2taxid.tsv
```
That will remove the version from the `acc2taxid.tsv` file. You don't need to recreate the fasta indexes nor to download the sequences again.
Also, since strain TaxIDs are deprecated since a while, my advice is to use `$7` instead of `$6` in the awk command, so that all the genomes will be linked to their species node (unless you are interested in strain-level classification, of course).

Concerning the parameters, `library_name` is the name of the subdirectory where the sequences are downloaded (e.g. bacteria, archaea, viral for the standard DBs, or any name of your choice), which will be prepended to the filenames in the tree. `library_main_dir` is the directory where all the sequences are downloaded, which usually is the parent of `library_name`. This should be used at indexing time (the `-g` parameter of `prophyle index`), unless the tree is placed in the very same directory.
Also these two parameters will be simplified, I understand they could be misleading.

The NCBI taxonomy is not downloaded automatically, unless the file `~/.etetoolkit/taxa.sqlite` does not exist. `ete3` is the library we rely upon to build the trees, and you can find some additional information here:
http://etetoolkit.org/docs/latest/tutorial/tutorial_ncbitaxonomy.html
In particular, to update the database using a specific version of the file `taxdump.tar.gz` distributed by NCBI, you can run the following commands in a python3 environment:
```
from ete3 import NCBITaxa
ncbi = NCBITaxa()
ncbi.update_taxonomy_database("path/to/taxdump.tar.gz")
```
This part is not well documented, but the code is here and the comments are quite clear:
https://github.com/etetoolkit/ete/blob/master/ete3/ncbi_taxonomy/ncbiquery.py

I hope that helps. Please tell me if you still have problems with it, and thanks for reporting this.
Simone

Hello,

The `clientCustomerId` is not tied to your credentials; if it is provided, it is sent along in the SOAP headers in your API requests.

You can use the [`LoadFromStorage`](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads/adwords.py#L250) method to load a [configuration file](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads.yaml#L10), by default it will load from your home directory. As you may have noticed, the configuration file allows you to set a `client_customer_id` field. Of course, if you're configuring the client manually, it is also possible to provide this as a [keyword argument](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads/adwords.py#L289).

Regards,
Mark

Hello,

Thanks for the report! I can see how this might not be super clear--the warning you're seeing is referring to your OS's locale's encoding. The reason this warning was added was because users without a locale using utf-8 had frequently reported that they were having some sort of encoding issue when the underlying issue was that their OS could not actually display certain characters because of their configuration.

This warning is just a harmless reminder that you're running on an environment that may not be able to display certain unicode characters. You can stop the nagging by configuring your OS to use a locale with utf-8 encoding.

Regards,
Mark

# TLDR

Hey, I'm having the same issue, but found a hacky workaround - see SOLUTION beneath if your tldr ;) 

# ANALYSIS

To dive into it I have the simple test server

```
def hello(environ, start_response):
    data = b"Hello World!"
    response_headers = [('Content-type','text/plain'),
                        ('Content-Length', str(len(data)))]
    start_response("200 OK", response_headers)
    return [data]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    wsgi.WSGIServer(hello).start()
```

when it starts via ```python3 app.py``` , I get

```
16:40:21 [p=383, t=139677125908224, INFO, pulsar.arbiter] mailbox serving on 127.0.0.1:42675
16:40:21 [p=383, t=139677125908224, INFO, pulsar.arbiter] started
16:40:21 [p=383, t=139677125908224, INFO, pulsar.wsgi] started
16:40:21 [p=385, t=139677125908224, INFO, pulsar.wsgi.worker] wsgi serving on 127.0.0.1:8060
16:40:21 [p=385, t=139677125908224, INFO, pulsar.wsgi.worker] started
```
My docker is basically run with ```docker -p 8060:8060 -p 5000:5000 -it myimage```.  So the port 8060 is open. (5000 is just to show that flask works later)

Inside the docker
```
$ curl localhost:8060
Hello World!
```

Outside the docker
```
$ curl localhost:8060
curl: (52) Empty reply from server
$ curl localhost:5000
curl: (52) Empty reply from server
$ curl localhost:5001
curl: (7) Failed to connect to localhost port 5001: Connection refused
```
When I then run a hello world in flask on 5000 everithing works fine.
Outside the docker
```
$ curl localhost:5000
Hello world
```

So it seems that somehow to the outsite world it is if there were no one listening to the 8060 port.

Now when I run it with `python3 app.py --bind localhost:8060` the output is a bit different...

```
17:27:46 [p=1056, t=140084304557824, INFO, pulsar.arbiter] mailbox serving on 127.0.0.1:43803
17:27:46 [p=1056, t=140084304557824, INFO, pulsar.arbiter] started
17:27:46 [p=1056, t=140084304557824, INFO, pulsar.wsgi] started
17:27:46 [p=1059, t=140084304557824, INFO, pulsar.wsgi.worker] wsgi serving on [::1]:8060
17:27:46 [p=1059, t=140084304557824, INFO, pulsar.wsgi.worker] wsgi serving on 127.0.0.1:8060
17:27:46 [p=1059, t=140084304557824, INFO, pulsar.wsgi.worker] started
```

... but the result stays the same.

# SOLUTION

Now when inside the docker I run 

```
$ ip route show
default via 172.17.0.1 dev eth0
172.17.0.0/16 dev eth0  proto kernel  scope link  src 172.17.0.2
```

Now when I run it with `python3 app.py --bind 172.17.0.2:8060` there comes remedy.

On the outside I get
```
$ curl localhost:8060
Hello World!
```

Yeay!

On the inside of the container I get

```
$ curl localhost:8060
curl: (7) Failed to connect to localhost port 8060: Connection refused
```

and even

```
$ curl 172.17.0.2:8060
curl: (7) Failed to connect to 172.17.0.2 port 8060: Connection refused
```

but luckily

```
$ curl 172.17.0.1:8060
Hello World!
```

# ANALYSIS

I'm no expert on networks but so far my interpretation is this: Docker seems to set up some sort of extra routes layer between the containers localhost and the outer world.

Every other web framework I know (flask, node, webapp2, apache, ...) binds both localhost and this extra layer by default and works with docker out of the box.

Pulsar so far binds only either or. Imho, this should be either changed (to always binding both) or added as an extra option.

# FURTHER READING

I got the idea from this [SO post]
(http://stackoverflow.com/questions/31324981/how-to-access-host-port-from-docker-container)

To be honest, I haven't quite used `max_id`, `min_id` and friends. I rely on a different set of offsets:

Yes, the current implementation requires convolving both coefficients again with the filter, and adding the two coefficients together and dividing by 2. The result will be circularly shifted by `2**(level-1)`, which is easy to correct for.

@egede I said _exacerbated_ by #164

We _may_ have loaded a bad job at some point or something has gone wrong in loading a job (other bug we __need__ to track down) but once we've loaded a bad job into memory ganga (on exit) will flush the job as it has been loaded to disk.

To be clear the consequence of #164 is that if you fail to fully load a job but ganga thinks it loaded (somehow) Ganga __WILL__ overwrite this job on exit with whatever is in memory (usually the default hello world job).

Hopefully this is another instance of the jobstatus being bad.

It is an issue with any castor based storage. The reason is that castor understands 'root' as the 'castor' protocol. in LHCbDIRAC, when root or xroot is required, we always add both, and make sure xroot is before root.

So there is a possible solution which adds log handler
```diff
diff --git a/pytest_twisted/plugin.py b/pytest_twisted/plugin.py

         if greenlet.getcurrent() is not current:
             current.switch(result)
 
+    def error_observer(eventDict):
+        if eventDict['isError'] and 'failure' in eventDict:
+            if not eventDict.get('why'):
+                failure = eventDict['failure']
+                if greenlet.getcurrent() is not current:
+                    current.throw(failure.type, failure.value, failure.tb)
+
+    from twisted.python import log
+    log.addObserver(error_observer)
```

The "derived" one-period return is what is called the one-period rate of return:  the returns the factor quantile would have had every 1-period if it had grown at a steady rate. 

But why does Alphalens computes the 1-period rate of return instead of computing the cumulative returns from a N-periods returns? Alphalens makes an assumption on how the factor should be used, that is how and how often a portfolio should be  rebalanced.

If the factor has the best performance at N periods (N >1) then a naive approach of using that factor would be to rebalance the portfolio every N periods. That would work but also it would make the portfolio performance dependent on the specific starting point. Also, Alphalens statistics are calculated considering the factor performance at each period, but if the factor is traded every N periods (rebalancing every N periods) we cannot expect to achieve the same performance.

So, the cumulative returns computed from N periods returns, are calculated assuming an algorithm that divides the initial capital by N and trade each 1/N sub-capital at subsequent periods and each sub-capital is then rebalanced every N periods. This means the algorithm would trade every period (one of the sub-capitals need to be rebalanced  every period) instead of every N-periods and this would mimic the cumulative returns you can see on Alphalens. Also, this kind of approach would produce lower volatility, better leverage/exposure control than an algorithm that rebalanced every N periods

Not proper portable. Ask for the installation type, asks to download snapshots, has installer.

That's not **portable**, that's custom installer.

#611 covers the case for the Windows installer and possibly the portable Windows version. However, it doesn't cover the pip (Linux/macOS/Windows) installation methods. It also means that the line has to be removed from the configuration to re-enable the auto version check. 

I'm more in favour of suppressing the current `no-version-check` option and adding an explicit `auto-version-check=yes|no` option, which defaults to `no`.  That way if we want to change the default back to `yes` we can do so without a new option (like now). If the default if `no` then `no-version-check` will have no effect, or we could make `no-version-check` an alias for `auto-version-check=no`,  for continued future compatibility but still removing `no-version-check` for help/docs.

Indeed, adding "timeout=self.timeout" to open_channel does raise an exception in SSHClient:

You need a "recent" enough version of paramiko that supports this option.
For example 1.15.2 did not and I had to upgrade.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./test_client.py", line 20, in <module>
    num_retries=1)
  File "/home/harter/tmp/test_parallel_ssh/pssh/ssh_client.py", line 127, in __init__
    self._connect_tunnel()
  File "/home/harter/tmp/test_parallel_ssh/pssh/ssh_client.py", line 149, in _connect_tunnel
    timeout=self.timeout)
  File "/home/harter/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/paramiko/transport.py", line 841, in open_channel
    raise SSHException('Timeout openning channel.')
paramiko.ssh_exception.SSHException: Timeout openning channel.
```
However it is not currently handled in ParallelSSHClient:

```
./test_client.py 
No handlers could be found for logger "pssh"
{}
0:00:01.594286
```

With this however, the connection time could be `2 x timeout` in the end as there are two connections.
So maybe the second timeout for connecting to the host should be `(timeout - elapsed_time)`.

@oshtaier There are a lot of entities that are available at more than one path. These API calls are synonymous:

* GET /docker/api/v2/containers
* GET /docker/api/v2/compute_resources/:compute_resource_id/containers

Similarly, these four paths are synonymous:

* GET /api/domains
* GET /api/subnets/:subnet_id/domains
* GET /api/locations/:location_id/domains
* GET /api/organizations/:organization_id/domains

These two paths are also synonymous:

* POST /katello/api/host_collections
* POST /katello/api/organizations/:organization_id/host_collections

Whenever you see "foo/:foo_id" in a path, you can mentally drop that path segment. That piece of information may somehow be necessary when making an API call (either as part of the URL path, the URL query or as a JSON payload) but it doesn't identify which entity is being worked with.

Sorry, Myke -- just seeing this now.

In this case, I think the data is wrong and the behaviour of the parser could be right. MEI makes a distinction between 'logical' and 'gestural' accidentals, which makes the case of no accidentals ambiguous in the data. 

A 'gestural' accidental is one that is performed but not written. In the case of a key signature with a B flat, one assumption is that every B has an implicit, performed-but-not-written accidental. This is because in MEI we do not capture the "pitch" of a note; rather, the "pitch name." So if you say: `<note pname="b" />` while in the key of F you're (technically) wrong, as you're saying that the note in question is a B, while it's actually a B flat, which should be `<note pname="b" accid.ges="f" />`. (It's not unlike when I have been corrected when asked to read out notes in a score. I would often ignore the key signature when saying the pitch name and be promptly corrected...)

If you're in the key of C, and it's a written B flat, it would be `<note pname="b" accid="f" />`. 

Unfortunately, this remains a point needing clarification by many MEI implementers. We're attempting to address this by specifically encoding in the file what the assumed 'domain' is when encoding, but this will be available in MEI 4.0.

Hey, thanks for checking out Trio! A few different thoughts here.

First, you should be aware of @smurfix's [trio-asyncio library](https://trio-asyncio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/), which lets you run asyncio code under trio and call back and forth, all in a single thread. (And in particular, using trio-asyncio means that you have the normal [nice cooperative scheduling guarantees](https://glyph.twistedmatrix.com/2014/02/unyielding.html) even if the trio code and asyncio code are both touching the same data structures. Using two threads can work too, but it means now you're doing thread programming with everything that comes with that.)

Second, regarding the question in the title of your issue, there's a little example here of how you might use trio and `concurrent.futures` together: https://gist.github.com/njsmith/be24d376faea61cb3999f08318b780f3 (untested). I *don't* think this is really relevant to your actual goal, but mentioning it anyway out of general interest and in case people looking for that start finding this issue through search engines :-).

Finally, for the real question, about calling back and forth between a trio thread and an asyncio thread. This is definitely doable. It's also a lot of gnarly glue code, because we're dealing with threads, and two different async libraries, and trying to adapt between their different abstractions. What makes it particularly tricky is hooking up their cancellation systems, so that if we cancel a call on the asyncio side then it propagates that to the trio side and vice-versa. It's an interesting challenge actually! If we want cancellation to work properly then I don't think we can use `concurrent.futures.Future`, but that's OK; we can use similar techniques to how `BlockingTrioPortal` is implemented, but using `loop.call_soon_threadsafe` / `token.run_sync_soon` to get back and forth instead of using blocking calls.

Anyway, I took a shot at it, and I think the basic approach should work: https://gist.github.com/njsmith/14502e2c41bcbdc409c3d5bc9f49a5b5. Unfortunately it's untested because that's as far as I got and I'm going to bed now, but if asyncio-trio doesn't work for you then maybe give it a try and let us know how it goes :-).

Actually, using `Result.acapture` in that example is wrong, because it'll swallow `KeyboardInterrupt` and `Cancelled` exceptions. We want something more like a conventional catch-all handler, like `except Exception`. And conventionally that would mean it needs to be replaced by `MultiError.catch`. Here we only really want to catch the `Exception` part of the `MultiError`, but in the common case where that's the whole `MultiError`, we want to preserve it as a single `MultiError` that we can pack into an `Error` object and add to the results list. Hmm.

I wonder if we should add something like a `MultiError.split` primitive that divides a `MultiError` into two pieces, those that match the predicate and those that don't... And maybe a `catch_split` built on it that catches all exceptions of a given class and hands them all at once to a handler? These might actually be the primitives we want for both cancel scopes and the log-and-discard-errors cases.

See also #285, #59, #408, ...

Hmm, looking at this again, and your PR, I guess I see how it could work after all. It's making my brain hurt a bit though :-).

Basically the way it's written, if a background task in a fixture crashes, then it causes the actual test to raise Cancelled, and then ... we absorb that exception. (As currently written I'm not sure it handles KeyboardInterrupt properly, but we can check.) In general in Trio this is totally wrong, you can't throw away `Cancelled` exceptions, but in this case we know we're about to exit so...... I guess maybe we can get away with it? In particular we know that any `Cancelled` exceptions *must* be triggered by cancel scopes inside the fixtures, which we're in the middle of closing anyway, because we know that we just called `trio.run` ourselves. So we know that the test has finished with some kind of error status, and that the `Cancelled` exceptions were going to be discarded while we went through our fixtures...

I guess there are two places where not threading the exceptions through properly causes some difference in effects:

- Normally, it makes a difference whether you exit a nursery via an exception or not, because the exception triggers cancellation of everything else in the nursery. But in this case any fixture that has a nursery had better be cancelling everything in it *anyway*, because that's what it means to tear down a fixture, so maybe the difference is not a big deal in practice?

- Normally, it a cancel scope records whether it caught an exception (in its `.cancelled_caught` attribute), which is used to implement things like `fail_after`. In this case I can't see any case where you'd actually care about the accuracy of this attribute though...

This all pretty confusing though. And one of the things I realized here: in practice, when you have two fixtures and they each make their own nursery, they end up coupled *anyway*, because if the fixture that happens to arbitrarily be treated as the "outer" nursery crashes, then the other nursery is automatically cancelled. (Plus of course they're also coupled by the fact that they're both being set up for the same test, and both will torn down at the same time regardless.)

So I'm still wondering if it might not end up being simpler all around to stick with a `nursery_fixture` fixture that all the fixtures are supposed to use. In practice this probably simplifies most fixtures too, because it means you don't have to keep reimplementing the cancel-on-teardown logic, i.e. compare:

```python
async def server_fixture():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.start_soon(whatever)
        yield
        nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
```

versus

```python
def server_fixture(nursery_fixture):
    nursery_fixture.start_soon(whatever)
```

I'm going to sleep on it :-). Either way I'm glad we're having this discussion, this is super confusing and digging into it like this is the only way to bring some clarity and hopefully get the best final result...

@chrishales709, SAS Studio is a very different setup.  In this case, the Studio Application maintains the Workspace connection while managing the connect/disconnect/timeout/reconnect between itself and the client you are using.  Should the Studio Application itself loose Workspace connectivity, it could not recover.  SASPy and Enterprise Guide behave similarly, as they are managing the connection directly from the clients, instead of connecting through a mid-tier.  SASPy could follow a similar paradigm.  Where the Java piece of SASPy, which manages the IOM connection is running on the SAS server (remote or otherwise) and then talking to Python, as it does not, through sockets, from the SAS server to the SASPy client and handling session timeout/reconnect from the SASPy server to the SASPy client...

Hi,

The equation in 
http://keisan.casio.com/exec/system/1224772199
is a transformed version of the original
F = -k * x - c * dx/dt
equation.

In here the c is the viscous damping coefficient. The btGeneric6DofSpring2Constraint uses this.

You have to transform your resistance coefficient (Kappa)

c = Kappa / (2 * m)

However I think it is generally better to use the damping ratio (zeta), because in this case:
zeta > 1 -> overdamped
zeta = 1 -> critically damped
zeta < 1 -> underdamped

c = zeta * (2 * sqrt(m * k))

As for the frequency that you wrote is correct:

k = omega0 ^ 2 * m


Please note that while the analytic equation is the perfect solution for the above differential equation, the Bullet can't use this because other forces and constraints have to be considered.
So the result is an approximation.
The good news is: if you set up the k and c values correctly the solution of the Bullet is very close for the analytic solution. (even with the original SI/PGS solver)
(My colleague Tamas Ummenhoffer made small example with a few different spring solvers and the Bullet's solution was the closest.)
Obviously you will never be able got a perfect solution (but that would be impossible in computer calculation world anyway)

You can always increase a precision by decreasing the delta t of the Bullet or play with the solver. (change it to Dijkstra, NNCG or increase the iteration count if it has)


Let me know if it solves your problem.

Best Regards:

Cool, this is going in the right direction!

I think it's better to have to do a bit more boilerplate than having stuff magically done for you. The confusion me and my colleagues had with the reactive framework is that a lot happens in the background and it isn't always clear what exactly happens. The conversations framework for example is really handy but it is hard to "get" because of the magic. For that reason I think that clarity should be one of our main concerns. The reactive framework is pretty powerful already, we just need to make it easier to use and "get".

To illustrate what I mean by "magic".

```python
@when('relation.mysql.database.available')
def update_database(mysql):
    #...
```

Who passes the mysql argument? Why do some states result in arguments passed and some don't? What is the order of multiple arguments? What is the object in the argument exactly?

@johnsca's proposal removes the magic. Now the developer is in control and it's clear where the flags come from and what the object is.

```python
@when('relation.mysql.database.available')
def update_database():
    mysql = flag('relation.mysql.database.available')
```

Although I would change the name of the function. `flag()` looks a bit to much like a constructor. You're not creating a flag, you're getting an already created flag. The terminology should make this clear, for example `get_flag()` and `set_flag()`.

- It might be best to explicitly disallow getting flags in a handler that doesn't use the flag in its preconditions (`@when`). This way we can save devs from shooting in their own foot.
- This will also solve a minor linting issue where pylint complains if you react to a relation state but don't use that state in the handler.

Regarding the MS-API for bookmarks:

* Good call on limiting scope here to bookmarks. That will be plenty of scope for the time being I think :)
* It looks like the MS-API makes a `Bookmarks` collection available on all of `Document`, `Range`, and `Selection`. I think we only need `Document` for now. We don't have the concept of `Range` or `Selection` in `python-docx`. (`Range` we may end up having at some point, `Selection` probably not because it corresponds to the state of a running Word application.)
* I don't see an immediate need for a `.copy()` method. I'm open to opposing views, but I don't see the immediate use case.
* I would place `.delete()` or perhaps `.remove()` in the "maybe later" category, unless you see a clear use case for it. It doesn't seem like it would be hard to implement, but I think we want to jealously limit scope for the time being. These things have a tendency to grow beyond one's ambition to complete them :)
* A `.select()` method doesn't make any sense for `python-pptx` for the "state in running application" reason I mentioned earlier.
* Of the ten properties, I would focus only on `.name`, possibly also including `.parent`, and maybe `.empty` if we think anyone cares about those two. `.Application`, `.Creator`, `.End`, `.Range`, and `.Start` all refer to concepts that don't exist in `python-docx`. `.StoryType` might be possible but I just don't see an active use for that. So my list shortens to 6, with 4 and 7 as possibly nice-to-haves.
* The lack of a *range* concept in `python-docx` though, leaves use without any interface to get to the "contents" of a bookmark. That probably bears some thought. We could implement `Bookmark.paragraphs`, although we'd have to work out what to do with a "partial" first or last paragraph. This one should go on the open noodle list.
* There definitely needs to be a `Bookmarks` object, available, I'm thinking, on `Document.bookmarks`. I'm inclined to believe it would need hybrid semantics, like some combination of sequence and mapping (list and dict). It would need to be iterable, it would have a length (support `.len()`), and I'm thinking indexed access (e.g. bookmarks[7]) would have sequence semantics. Key lookup (by name) could be with a `.get()` method, with the same interface as that method on a dict (e.g. optional second parameter to specify default return value). We could think about a "lookup by ID" method if we thought that would be needed, maybe `.get_by_id(id)`. Along those lines, the analysis should determine whether bookmark names are enforced unique and what the lookup behavior is when two have the same name. I think it's safe to assume bookmark IDs are unique.

To ease reviewing, here's a copy of the documentation for the new feature:

## Hostname in lock file

In a container environment (e.g. Docker), the PID is typically always
identical even if multiple containers are running under the same operating
system instance.

Clearly, inspecting lock files doesn't then help much in debugging. To identify
the container which created the lock file, we need information about the
container in the lock file. Since Docker uses the container identifier or name
as the hostname, this information can be stored in the lock file in addition to
or instead of the PID.

Use the `content_template` keyword argument to `LockFile` to specify a
custom lock file content format:

    >>> lock = zc.lockfile.LockFile('lock', content_template='{pid};{hostname}')
    >>> lock.close()

If you now inspected the lock file, you would see e.g.:

    $ cat lock
     123;myhostname

@sliverc I'll start a PR shortly.  Just a few items to get your feedback:

- [ ] The filter implementation should be Djangoistic (e.g. use the various django-filter backends, queries via the ORM, etc.
- [ ] It should be a relatively simple to use a `filter` which the jsonapi spec intentionally does not define. So it's a "use this at your option for lack of a better one"; we can't claim it complies with the spec because there is none other than this statement: "[The filter query parameter is reserved for filtering data. Servers and clients SHOULD use this key for filtering operations.](http://jsonapi.org/format/#fetching-filtering)" However, there are also some [recommendations](http://jsonapi.org/recommendations/#filtering) that lead to the use of `filter[stuff]`.
- [ ] It should properly map between model representation and DJA representations of field names (use `JSON_API_FORMAT_FIELD_NAMES`).
- [ ] Should it map django orm use of `__` (double U+005F LOW LINE, “_”) some other way since double `__` is a [legal component of a member name](http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-member-names-allowed-characters)? So that in djangoistic style one would write `filter[courses__course_terms__term_identifier]=20181` perhaps it should use ["U+002E PERIOD, “.” (used as a separator within relationship paths)"](http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-member-names-reserved-characters): `filter[courses.courses_terms.term_identifier]=20181`.
- [ ] How do we have _all the things we like about the ORM_ such as `__gt` or `__in` in a filter? `filter[courses.course_terms.term_identfier.gt]=2018`? Is that still a `relationship path` component. Since filters are not defined by the spec, I would argue that this still follows the style of the spec.
- [ ] It must return a 400 status for unknown query parameters (["If a server encounters a query parameter that does not follow the naming conventions above, and the server does not know how to process it as a query parameter from this specification, it MUST return 400 Bad Request."](http://jsonapi.org/format/#query-parameters)). With the current approach. unknown query parameters are currently just silently ignored.
- [ ] Should it be a single backend filter that one can include in `REST_FRAMEWORK['DEFAULT_FILTER_BACKENDS']` or just documented usage of multiple filters as it is (mostly) now? For example, include all these filters in a single `JsonApiFilterBackend`:
       -  `rest_framework.filters.OrderingFilter` and `ordering_param = 'sort'`
       -  `django_filters.rest_framework.DjangoFilterBackend` to use `filter[field]` query parameter
       -  `rest_framework.filters.SearchFilter` to use `search_param=filter[all]`
      Probably keep them separate to allow the most configurability for the developer, I think. Maybe with a better way to override the `REST_FRAMEWORK` defaults?

http://jsonapi.org/format/1.0/#document-resource-object-linkage

```
Resource Linkage

Resource linkage in a compound document allows a client to link together all of the included resource objects without having to GET any URLs via links.

Resource linkage MUST be represented as one of the following:

    null for empty to-one relationships.
    an empty array ([]) for empty to-many relationships.
    ...
```

so a "**compound**" (see also #11) document should represent resource *linkages* in the format that this pull request achieves (pending your verification!).

A.  first byte  is un unsigned 8 bit integer representing the number of bytes in the SP field ( including the first one )

C. third is indicating item category

If you pass it as an environment variable it will work 100% sure. A similar solution and maybe even better could be the following.

1. Mocked data is moved to ``$vendor/mocked_data/$testname_$id/``
2. The test method loops over all the subfolders of  ``$vendor/mocked_data/`` that start with its name.

So, for example, we would have folder:

* ``junos/mocked_data/test_get_lldp_neighbors_00/``
* ``junos/mocked_data/test_get_lldp_neighbors_01/``

So the test method would discover that there are two different subtests it has to run for the same test.

In this case we don't have to pass any argument to the test.

If you want to be able to handle errors, you need to keep track of the future returned by `subscribe` and call `result()` on it, for example:

```
future = self.subscriber.subscribe(...)
future.result() # blocks forever or until an exception occurs and then raises that exception
```
